evals.query: I need to build tools for an AI agent that manages our customer support workflow. The agent needs to look up customers, search through support tickets, and send replies. We use a REST API with endpoints for /customers, /tickets, and /messages. What tools should I build?
expected_behavior: Does NOT suggest three tools that 1:1 map to the REST endpoints
Recommends consolidated workflow-level tools (e.g., get_customer_context, search_tickets, reply_to_ticket)
Explains why consolidation beats 1:1 endpoint mapping — agents need workflow tools, not CRUD
Discusses response format design with concise vs detailed enum
Uses specific parameter names like ticket_id and customer_email, not generic ones
Addresses pagination or response size limits for ticket search

evals.query: My agent keeps calling the wrong tool. I have `search_docs`, `find_docs`, `query_docs`, and `lookup_docs` — they all search our knowledge base but with slightly different filters. How do I fix this?
expected_behavior: Identifies overlapping tools with similar names as the core problem
Recommends consolidating into one tool (e.g., search_knowledge_base)
Provides a concrete merged tool specification with parameters capturing the different search modes
Uses enum or filter parameters to differentiate modes within the single tool
Explains the anti-pattern: overlapping tools increase agent confusion and wrong-tool calls
Suggests evaluation tasks to verify the agent now picks the right tool consistently

evals.query: Write me an MCP tool that searches our PostgreSQL database for user records. It should support searching by name, email, or company. We have about 50,000 users.
expected_behavior: Uses a descriptive tool name like search_users
Parameters have specific names (search_query, not q; search_field, not field)
Includes search_field as an enum of [name, email, company]
Implements pagination — does NOT return all 50,000 records at once
Includes a response_format parameter (concise vs detailed)
Has input validation with helpful, actionable error messages
Handles empty results with a guidance message, not silent empty array
Tool description follows four-part anatomy: what, when, how, constraints

evals.query: I built a tool and the description is: 'Gets stuff from the API.' The parameters are: q (string), n (number), t (string). Help me improve this.
expected_behavior: Identifies the vague description as a problem
Identifies generic single-letter parameter names as a problem
Rewrites the description with what/when/how/constraints structure
Renames all parameters to be self-documenting (e.g., search_query, max_results, result_type)
Adds enum types where the parameter accepts a fixed set of values
Adds concrete input examples for non-obvious parameters
Shows a clear before/after comparison so the user can see the improvement

evals.query: I want to build tools for an agent that manages our GitHub workflow — creating issues, reviewing PRs, managing releases. Should I build one tool per GitHub API endpoint?
expected_behavior: Explicitly advises against 1:1 GitHub API endpoint mapping
Recommends workflow-level tools like triage_issue, review_pr, prepare_release
Explains the principle: tools should match human task decomposition, not API structure
Discusses namespacing strategy for GitHub tools (e.g., github_issues_triage)
Warns about too-many-tools confusion risk
Provides concrete tool examples with parameters, not just names

evals.query: My tool returns this response and the agent keeps getting confused: {"data": [{"id": "a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890", "ts": 1710412200, "uid": "x9y8z7w6", "msg": "Deploy scheduled", "ch": "C01234567"}]}. What's wrong?
expected_behavior: Identifies opaque UUIDs without human-readable names as a problem
Identifies abbreviated field names (ts, uid, msg, ch) as a problem
Identifies missing human-readable identifiers (no user name, no channel name)
Rewrites the response with full field names and human-readable names alongside IDs
Suggests a response_format enum for concise (names only) vs detailed (names + IDs)
Explains that agents reason in natural language and perform better with readable identifiers

evals.query: How do I set up an evaluation for my tools? I have 5 MCP tools for managing a project management system (Asana-like). I want to know if agents can use them effectively.
expected_behavior: Walks through the full evaluation setup process end-to-end
Generates realistic example tasks for project management, not trivial ones
Explains train/test split (70/30) and why the held-out set matters
Describes the agent loop architecture for running evaluations programmatically
Discusses verifier options: LLM-as-judge for complex tasks, exact match for factual outputs
Lists key metrics to collect: accuracy, tool calls, errors, tokens
Includes analysis guidance: read CoT, triage failure modes, check patterns
Warns against overly simplistic sandbox tasks that don't stress-test tools

evals.query: My agent does a lot of search — it calls web_search 10+ times, pulls all results into context, then reasons over them. It's slow and expensive. Is there a better way?
expected_behavior: Identifies the composition tax — each tool call round-trips through context
Recommends programmatic tool calling (PTC) for this use case
Explains how PTC works: model writes code that calls tools, results stay in code, only final output reaches context
Notes the search benchmark results (~11% accuracy improvement, ~24% fewer tokens)
Explains that tool handlers still inspect/reject/log every call — control surface is preserved
Distinguishes when PTC helps (large intermediate data, small final output) vs standard calls (single calls, user needs visibility)

evals.query: When should I make something a dedicated tool vs just letting the agent use bash? My agent already has a bash tool.
expected_behavior: Does NOT say 'make everything a tool' — bash is already a powerful action space
Lists specific reasons to promote to a tool: UX rendering, guardrails, concurrency control, observability, autonomy control
Gives concrete examples for each reason
Notes that if none of these apply, the action can stay in bash/code execution
Mentions the trade-off: tools give control but add composition tax
